nodes.label: Energy & Entropy
nodes.summary: All organised systems require energy to maintain organisation while entropy continuously degrades structure.

nodes.label: The Triad
nodes.summary: Gradient, order, and constraint — the mechanism by which energy becomes organised into stable structure.

nodes.label: Fundamental Intrinsic Properties
nodes.summary: Define what can physically exist — how matter, force, material, and geometry behave.

nodes.label: Complex Intrinsic Properties
nodes.summary: Describe how organised systems behave through time: emergence, scaling, feedback, thresholds, adaptation, regulation.

nodes.label: Intrinsic Emergence
nodes.summary: The threshold at which structure can begin to appear from organised energy.

nodes.label: Neurophysical System
nodes.summary: How living organisms perceive, regulate, and respond to their environment through sensory processing and movement.

nodes.label: Human Conditions
nodes.summary: Universal biological and psychological needs: light, shelter, warmth, prospect and refuge, sensory texture.

nodes.label: Sublimation
nodes.summary: The process through which human instinct, emotion, and social behaviour are transformed into symbolic, cultural, and spatial structure.

nodes.label: The Dynamic Engine
nodes.summary: How coherence unfolds through six phases: Translational, Morphogenesis, Emergence, Becoming, Unfolding, Continuous Improvement.

nodes.label: Coherence Hierarchy
nodes.summary: Six domains of coherence: Aesthetic/Structural, Dynamic, Livingness, Societal, Symbolic, Connection to Nature.

nodes.label: Centres
nodes.summary: Local concentrations of organised relationship — the primary units from which forms, patterns, and systems develop.

nodes.label: Congruence
nodes.summary: Local integration of strong or compatible centres within a limited area.

nodes.label: Wholeness
nodes.summary: Multiple forms of coherence converge into an integrated, surrounding, and mutually reinforcing whole.

nodes.label: Endurance
nodes.summary: The capacity of a system to continue through time without losing viability, coherence, or meaning.

nodes.label: Pattern Languages
nodes.summary: Recurring spatial, social, and organisational relationships that allow systems to work well and endure.

nodes.label: Form Languages
nodes.summary: The geometric, material, and spatial means through which coherent relationships are physically expressed.

nodes.label: Perceptive Emergence
nodes.summary: The human recognition and experience of structure as coherent, meaningful, beautiful, or alive.

nodes.label: Alignment
nodes.summary: Compatibility between physical, biological, human, and temporal constraints. The mechanism through which coherence is produced.

nodes.label: Coherence
nodes.summary: The condition under which a system can sustain alignment under constraint without exhausting its means.
